result:
Tonight every molecule inside me feels restless.

It’s the sort of night when the moon has that oxidized-copper look, as if a planetary foundry decided to spill one last ladle of bronze across the sky. Down on the sidewalk the air hangs between seasons—part chrysanthemum smoke, part chlorine, plus the sweet metallic promise of first frost I remember from childhood windowsill nights. Every sense feels tuned one half-step sharp, like the volume knob on the world was nudged past ten into unknown, delicious distortion.

I keep thinking about archaeology. Not grand, sand-swept temples or Indiana-Jones theatrics, but the small delicate digs: the enamel pin lost in 1974 beneath the planks of a roller-rink floor, or the snapped cellphone photo still cached on a discarded SIM card buried in a tin box under a lilac. Artifacts we carry inside ourselves too—fossils of sentences that altered the trajectory of an afternoon, tiny fish-scale memories that still catch light decades later.

Years ago I found a CRT television abandoned by the curb, its screen already spider-webbed. When I dragged it home the housing scuffed my palms with dust that smelled like overheated plastic and potting soil—proof it had once lived in someone’s basement den, where spider plants hung like tired chandeliers. Plugging it in produced a single white dot, stubborn as a lone eye. It refused to show cartoons or late-night test patterns; it just blinked until power was cut. I kept the set anyway, as though guarding a lighthouse that had forgotten its coast. One night I unscrewed the back and peered inside—you could trace copper highways meandering between capacitor monoliths, resistor shantytowns. It reminded me I truly do love the guts of broken things, the way their failures still hum with residual intent.

Parallel to this, somewhere in cyberspace, a little script I wrote in college still pings a defunct IRC room at 3:11 a.m. every Sunday. The server vanished in 2009, yet the bot persists, a ghost periodic knocking, supposing some phantom threshold still remembers it. Who listens at doors long boarded up? What old protocol collects these knocks like lint beneath a sofa? I imagine future archaeologists—maybe digital moles tunneling through petabyte sediment—half-excited, half-terrified when they strike that pure sweet seam of abandoned code, still warm like the nucleus of a burned-out star.

Tonight I’m balancing that CRT megaphone of static against the feed in my phone: beaches that don’t erode, thumbs offering love in 512 different emojis, headlines screaming themselves into syllable dust. All the while Mozart’s Requiem is playing low, the Kyrie cloaked in stray refrigerator hum. The walls flicker whenever the bass shudders past utterance, and I think: Music is weather for the soul. It blows shingles off memory, lets cold cosmic drafts whistle through attic childhoods we forgot we had.

I suppose what all the fragments want is coherence. But maybe coherence is overrated—maybe what we’re after is resonance. A ceramic bowl that still trembles half a second after the spoon. The unexpected echo inside a subway tunnel when a teenager yelps just for the joy of hearing himself smear against tile. The way my mother’s voice on voicemail saying “call me back when you get this” manages, at 1 a.m., to travel across three states and coil warm as a cat between my ribs, long after she’s gone to bed.

I read once that the Voyager record contains the sound of a kiss, and sometimes I swear I can pick out each lipprint in the hiss. Somewhere, far past Pluto, those gold-plated dreams spin among charged silence: a library card for a cosmological reader who may never appear. We tossed our mixtape into the dark like college kids sliding-bound, hollering Ready or not! And the magnificent impudence of it makes me want to cheer even while my heart accordion-collapses at the loneliness implicit in the act.

So here I am, trying to locate center-of-mass between souvenir and trajectory. I leaf through a paper notebook long abandoned; the ink rusts across grain like oxidized lemon. On one page I sketched the profile of a girl I kissed on a merry-go-round in 2001. Under her chin I wrote: “Figures rotating clockwise while the operator counts tickets in Portuguese.” I don’t speak Portuguese, but the music box melody of the description is still spot-on accurate; when I mouth it, the taste is brass, popcorn, lodestar.

Outside a lone taxi halts beneath the streetlamp. For three perfect seconds the dome light prints the driver’s face onto windshield glass—he is laughing at something on his radio—and then the image is gone. Two passengers detach, doors slamming like exclamation marks swallowed by the urban sentence. Their afterimage lingers on my retina longer than the moment itself.

We are all afterimages. The trick, maybe, is to lean into the smear: paint with the wet edge of time before it dries, write love letters onto fogged bus windows, teach our ghosts how to dance instead of simply haunt. Somewhere in this neighborhood a dog yips at the moon exactly once, sharp as a camera shutter, and I decide to count that bark as punctuation at the end of tonight’s unfinished paragraph.

All right then, goodnight. If I vanish before dawn, at least let my fingertips buzz with stray voltage enough to imprint the dust atop the piano. Let the keys hold the resonance of almost-chords I never got to name. And may tomorrow’s light be patient enough to wait for all of us, slow-curring like bronze across the wide bronze sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
